quiz.q: What distinguished Macauliffe's approach from much earlier European writing on the Sikhs?
options: He wrote entirely in Punjabi
He sought to represent Sikhi authentically and reverently, working with Sikh scholars rather than judging it through a colonial lens

quiz.q: How did Macauliffe structure 'The Sikh Religion'?
options: As a single alphabetical encyclopedia
Around the lives of the Gurus, paired with the scriptural compositions and authors
Purely as a chronological war history

quiz.q: According to the course, how should Macauliffe's English renderings of Gurbani best be regarded?
options: As the authoritative scripture itself
As a respectful doorway into the meaning, not a substitute for the original

quiz.q: Which of the following is a fair limitation of Macauliffe's work that a modern student should keep in mind?
options: He had no contact with any Sikhs
It is a product of its time, with dated historical methods and an interpretive framing that should be supplemented by later scholarship
It was never read by anyone

quiz.q: What is the most appropriate posture for reading Macauliffe today, according to the course?
options: Reject the work entirely as worthless colonial writing
Accept every conclusion as final and beyond question
Read it appreciatively yet critically, honoring its achievement while updating it with later knowledge and the living tradition's authoritative sources
Use it only as a translation to replace the original scripture

courses.title: Miri-Piri: The Spiritual and the Temporal
courses.summary: A close study of the Sikh teaching that the spiritual and the worldly belong together: the two swords of Guru Hargobind Ji, the Akal Takht, the saint-soldier ideal, and what just power (Halimi Raj, Deg Teg) means in Sikh life.
outcomes: Explain in simple terms what Miri and Piri mean and why Sikhi joins them.
Describe the events of 1606 that gave rise to the teaching.
Explain the meaning of the two swords and the Akal Takht.
Describe the saint-soldier (Sant-Sipahi) ideal and its balance.
Explain Halimi Raj and Deg Teg as a model of just power.
Recognise how Miri-Piri is used well today and how it can be distorted.

terms.m: Worldly or temporal power.
Spiritual authority.
The 'Throne of the Timeless One'; seat of the community's worldly authority, built 1606.
The saint-soldier: a person both devoted to God and ready to protect others.
Rule built on humility, where the strong serve rather than crush.
'Pot and sword'; power must both feed (care) and protect.
Righteous duty and moral order.
Fearlessness; bravery without hatred.

references: Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Rev. ed. The New Cambridge History of India. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1998.
McLeod, W. H. The Sikhs: History, Religion, and Society. New York: Columbia University Press, 1989.
Kalra, Virinder. "Secular and Religious (Miri/Piri) Domains in Sikhism." In The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies, edited by Pashaura Singh and Louis E. Fenech. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2014.
Singh, Pashaura, and Louis E. Fenech, eds. The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2014.

lessons.title: Introduction: Why Miri-Piri Matters
lessons.summary: What the teaching is, and the questions it answers.
lessons.html:
Full course contents
  1. Introduction: Why Mīrī-Pīrī Matters
  2. The Historical Moment of 1606
  3. The Two Swords and the ਅਕਾਲ ਤਖ਼ਤ
  4. The Saint-Soldier (ਸੰਤ-ਸਿਪਾਹੀ)
  5. Sovereignty: ਹਲੀਮੀ ਰਾਜ and ਦੇਗ ਤੇਗ਼
  6. Mīrī-Pīrī Today: Use and Misuse

This course studies one of the most important ideas in Sikh thought: ਮੀਰੀ-ਪੀਰੀ — the teaching that worldly life and spiritual life are joined, not split apart. ਮੀਰੀ means worldly or temporal power. ਪੀਰੀ means spiritual authority. Sikhi holds that a good person must care for both: the soul and the world, prayer and justice.

Many religions ask people to leave the world to find God. The Sikh Gurus taught the opposite path. They taught that you stay in the world, work honestly, raise a family, and still keep God in your heart. Mīrī-Pīrī is the clearest form of this idea. It says a Sikh should be devoted to God and, at the same time, ready to stand up for what is right (Grewal 1998).

What this idea answers

QuestionThe Mīrī-Pīrī answer
Should holy people stay out of politics?No. Faith must also protect the weak and seek justice.
Is power dangerous to the soul?Power without ਧਰਮ (righteous duty) is dangerous; power used for service is good.
What is the ideal Sikh?The ਸੰਤ-ਸਿਪਾਹੀ — a saint and a soldier in one person.

In the lessons ahead we will see how this teaching was born in a hard moment of history, what the two swords and the ਅਕਾਲ ਤਖ਼ਤ mean, and how the idea guides Sikh life today.

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

lessons.title: The Historical Moment of 1606
lessons.summary: Guru Arjan's martyrdom and Guru Hargobind Ji's response.
lessons.html:
To understand Mīrī-Pīrī, we must look at the year 1606. In that year the fifth Guru, Guru Arjan Dev Ji, gave his life under Mughal rule. This was the first great martyrdom in Sikh history (McLeod 1989).

His young son, Guru Hargobind Ji, became the sixth Guru at this very moment. He saw that the community now needed to protect itself and the helpless. So he taught that faith and strength must walk together.

The setting in brief

Year (CE)Event
1606Martyrdom of Guru Arjan Dev Ji; Guru Hargobind Ji becomes Guru.
1606Guru Hargobind Ji takes up two swords and lays the foundation of the ਅਕਾਲ ਤਖ਼ਤ.

This was not a call to attack others. It was a call to be ready to defend ਧਰਮ — righteous duty and the right of all people to live in dignity (Kalra 2014).

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

lessons.title: The Two Swords and the Akal Takht
lessons.summary: The meaning of the swords and the throne facing the Harmandir Sahib.
lessons.html:
When Guru Hargobind Ji took up the Guruship, he wore two swords. One stood for ਪੀਰੀ (spiritual authority) and one for ਮੀਰੀ (worldly power). Together they say a simple truth: the Guru guides both the inner life and the outer life.

He then built the ਅਕਾਲ ਤਖ਼ਤ (the "Throne of the Timeless One") at Amritsar, facing the Harmandir Sahib. The original platform was raised by the Guru with Bhai Gurdas Ji and Baba Buddha Ji (Grewal 1998).

Two centres, two roles

PlaceStands forRole
Harmandir SahibਪੀਰੀPrayer, worship, the inner path.
ਅਕਾਲ ਤਖ਼ਤਮੀਰੀCommunity decisions, justice, worldly matters.

Placing the two side by side was itself the lesson: the spiritual and the worldly are partners, and true authority belongs to ਅਕਾਲ — the Timeless One alone (Kalra 2014).

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

lessons.title: The Saint-Soldier (Sant-Sipahi)
lessons.summary: Bravery joined to devotion; fearless but never hateful.
lessons.html:
The ideal person in this teaching is the ਸੰਤ-ਸਿਪਾਹੀ — the saint-soldier. This is someone whose heart is full of love for God (the saint) and who is also brave and ready to protect others (the soldier).

The "soldier" part does not mean anger or pride. It means ਨਿਰਭਉ (fearlessness) used for good. In the ਮੂਲ ਮੰਤਰ at the very start of Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji, God is described as ਨਿਰਭਉ (without fear) and ਨਿਰਵੈਰੁ (without hate). The saint-soldier tries to live by these same qualities: brave, but never full of hatred.

Two sides, one person

The saint (ਸੰਤ)The soldier (ਸਿਪਾਹੀ)
Prays and remembers GodProtects the weak
Humble and calmBrave and firm for justice
Free of ego (ਹਉਮੈ)Free of fear (ਨਿਰਭਉ)

So strength is always guided by prayer, and prayer is always ready to act. That balance is the heart of Mīrī-Pīrī (McLeod 1989).

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

lessons.title: Sovereignty: Halimi Raj and Deg Teg
lessons.summary: Just power that both feeds and protects.
lessons.html:
Mīrī-Pīrī also shapes how Sikhi thinks about power and rule. The Sikh ideal is not to grab power for oneself. It is ਹਲੀਮੀ ਰਾਜ — a rule built on humility, where the strong serve the weak instead of crushing them.

A second well-known pair of words is ਦੇਗ ਤੇਗ਼. ਦੇਗ is the cooking pot — it stands for feeding and caring for all people (as in ਲੰਗਰ). ਤੇਗ਼ is the sword — it stands for protecting people. Together they teach that real power must both feed and protect (Grewal 1998).

What just power looks like

SymbolMeaningIn practice
ਦੇਗCare and sharingFeed the hungry; serve all without caste or creed.
ਤੇਗ਼ProtectionDefend the weak; stand against cruelty.
ਹਲੀਮੀ ਰਾਜHumble ruleLead by service, not by pride.

Power, in this view, is a trust from ਅਕਾਲ. It is given so that ਧਰਮ can be kept alive (Kalra 2014).

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

lessons.title: Miri-Piri Today: Use and Misuse
lessons.summary: Living the balance, and guarding against distortion.
lessons.html:
Mīrī-Pīrī is still alive in Sikh life today. It is why Sikhs run free kitchens (ਲੰਗਰ), help in disasters, and speak up for human rights — while also keeping daily prayer (ਨਿਤਨੇਮ). The two swords still hang together.

But every strong idea can be misused. Mīrī-Pīrī is not a licence for anger, hatred, or grabbing power. The "Mīrī" side must always stay joined to the "Pīrī" side. When power forgets prayer and ਧਰਮ, it stops being Mīrī-Pīrī and becomes simple worldly ambition (Kalra 2014).

Keeping the balance

True Mīrī-PīrīA distortion of it
Strength used to serve and protectStrength used for ego or revenge
Power guided by prayer and ਧਰਮPower cut off from faith
Humble rule (ਹਲੀਮੀ ਰਾਜ)Pride and domination

In summary: Mīrī-Pīrī teaches that the soul and the world belong together. The Sikh ideal is the ਸੰਤ-ਸਿਪਾਹੀ: loving God, serving people, and standing for justice — all at once (Grewal 1998; McLeod 1989).

References: Grewal, The Sikhs of the Punjab (1998); Kalra, "Secular and Religious (Mīrī/Pīrī) Domains in Sikhism," in Singh and Fenech, eds., The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies (2014).

quiz.q: What does Miri-Piri teach?

quiz.q: What is the Akal Takht?

courses.title: The Five Ks and Sikh Identity
courses.summary: An introduction to the visible identity of the Khalsa and the five articles of faith known as the panj kakaar: Kesh, Kangha, Kara, Kachhera, and Kirpan. This course explains the spiritual meaning and practical purpose of each, situates them within the wider framework of Sikh discipline (Rehat) and initiation (Amrit Sanchar), and explores how a committed, accountable identity functions in daily life and in the modern world.

lessons.title: The Khalsa and the Idea of a Visible Identity
lessons.html:
What the Khalsa Identity Means

The word Khalsa refers to a community of Sikhs who have formally committed themselves to a life of faith, discipline, and service. In Sikh understanding, the Khalsa was given a distinct and recognizable form so that a person's inner devotion would be matched by an outer commitment that could be seen and lived in the open. To belong to the Khalsa is to accept a way of life rather than merely a label.

Faith Made Visible

Many traditions keep religious devotion private. Sikh teaching takes a different path: it asks the committed Sikh to carry the faith publicly, in dress and bearing as well as in conduct. This visibility is not for display or pride. It is meant to bind the believer to a standard of behavior, because a person who is recognizable as a Sikh is also accountable to act like one at all times.

A Saint and a Soldier

A recurring theme in Sikh thought is the ideal of the sant-sipahi, the saint-soldier, who unites spiritual depth with the courage to defend justice. The Khalsa identity expresses both sides of this ideal. It points inward to prayer, humility, and remembrance of the Divine, and outward to readiness, dignity, and the protection of the weak. The articles of faith you will study in this course are the physical expression of that balance.

Why an Outward Form Matters

Critics sometimes ask why outward symbols should matter if faith lives in the heart. The Sikh response is that human beings are shaped by their habits and surroundings. A consistent form supports a consistent character. It creates a daily, physical reminder of one's promises, and it knits individuals into a shared community whose members can recognize and support one another. In the lessons ahead, we will look at each of the Five Ks in turn, and then at the broader framework that gives them meaning.

lessons.title: Kesh and the Dastaar: Honoring the Natural Form
lessons.html:
Kesh: Unshorn Hair

The first of the Five Ks is Kesh, the practice of keeping the hair uncut. For a committed Sikh, all the hair of the body is left to grow naturally and is never trimmed or shaved. This includes the hair of the head and the beard. Kesh is among the most visible and deeply held of the articles of faith.

The Meaning of Keeping Kesh

Kesh expresses acceptance of the body in its natural, God-given form. Rather than altering the body to follow changing fashions, the Sikh chooses to live in harmony with the way one was created. In this sense Kesh is an act of humility and gratitude: it is a way of saying that the natural form is good as it is, and that the believer trusts the wisdom of the Creator over passing custom. It also represents discipline, since maintaining and caring for long hair requires daily attention and patience.

The Dastaar (Turban)

Closely connected to Kesh is the dastaar, or turban. The dastaar is wrapped over the head to keep the long hair clean and tidy, but it carries meaning far beyond practicality. Historically it was worn as a mark of dignity, sovereignty, and responsibility. By tying the dastaar, a Sikh declares a commitment to live uprightly and to be accountable in public for that commitment.

A Crown of Commitment

The turban is often described as a crown that any committed Sikh, regardless of social standing, may wear. In this way it carries a message of equality: dignity is not reserved for rulers or the wealthy but is the rightful bearing of every person who lives with integrity. To tie the dastaar each day is to renew that sense of responsibility. Together, Kesh and the dastaar form a strong and unmistakable part of the Sikh identity, joining respect for the natural form with a public pledge of honorable conduct.

lessons.title: Kangha: Discipline and Cleanliness
lessons.html:
Kangha: The Wooden Comb

The second article of faith is the Kangha, a small comb that is kept tucked in the hair beneath the turban. Although it is the simplest of the Five Ks in appearance, it carries an important lesson about how faith is meant to be lived in everyday detail.

Caring for the Kesh

The Kangha is used to keep the uncut hair clean, untangled, and well ordered. It is typically passed through the hair at least twice a day. In this practical sense, the comb is the natural companion of Kesh: the hair is not cut, but neither is it neglected. The Sikh is expected to keep the hair, and the body generally, clean and cared for.

Cleanliness as a Spiritual Value

The Kangha teaches that spirituality and orderly living belong together. Sikh thought does not separate the care of the body from the care of the soul; both call for discipline and regular attention. Just as the comb removes tangles from the hair, the believer is encouraged to remove disorder and impurity from the mind through prayer, honest living, and the remembrance of the Divine.

The Lesson of Daily Discipline

Because the Kangha is used every day, it stands for the value of steady, repeated effort. Faith is not built in a single dramatic moment but in small acts of care performed faithfully over a lifetime. The humble comb thus reminds the Sikh that a disciplined life is made of disciplined days. It also reflects a balance found throughout Sikh teaching: a person is to remain engaged in the world, presentable and active, rather than withdrawing from it. In the Kangha, a tiny everyday object becomes a quiet teacher of order, cleanliness, and constancy.

lessons.title: Kara: The Bond of the Steel Bracelet
lessons.html:
Kara: The Steel Bracelet

The third article of faith is the Kara, a circular bracelet usually made of iron or steel, worn on the wrist. Plain and unadorned, the Kara is rich in symbolism and is one of the most recognizable signs of a Sikh.

An Unbroken Circle

The Kara has no beginning and no end. This unbroken circle is understood as a reminder of the eternal nature of the Divine, which is without origin or limit. Wearing it on the wrist keeps this reminder constantly before the believer through the activity of the day. Whatever a Sikh sets out to do, the circle on the wrist recalls a connection to something timeless and complete.

A Reminder for the Hand

Because the Kara rests on the hand, the part of the body with which we act in the world, it is often understood as a check on conduct. When the hand moves to do something, the bracelet is there as a gentle prompt: let this action be honest, just, and in keeping with one's faith. In this way the Kara links belief to behavior, encouraging the wearer to do good work and to avoid wrongdoing.

Strength and Restraint

The choice of plain steel is itself meaningful. Steel is strong and durable, suggesting steadfastness and resilience in living one's values. At the same time, the Kara is deliberately simple, without jewels or ornament, reflecting humility rather than vanity. The bracelet therefore joins two ideas that run throughout Sikh life: inner strength and outward modesty. As an ever-present band on the wrist, the Kara quietly accompanies the Sikh in every task, a small circle that points toward an unlimited reality and toward a life of integrity.

lessons.title: Kachhera: Modesty and Self-Control
lessons.html:
Kachhera: The Cotton Undergarment

The fourth article of faith is the Kachhera, a specially designed cotton undergarment worn by committed Sikhs. Practical and modest, the Kachhera expresses several values that are central to a disciplined life.

A Garment of Readiness

The Kachhera is cut to allow free, comfortable movement. Historically this was important for those who needed to be active and ready at any moment, whether in work or in the defense of others. The garment thus reflects the Sikh ideal of being alert and prepared, able to respond to the needs of the day without hindrance.

Modesty and Dignity

The Kachhera is also a sign of modesty. By wearing it, the Sikh affirms the importance of decency and dignity in how one carries oneself. Modesty in Sikh teaching is not shame about the body but respect for it, and a refusal to treat the body as something merely to be displayed. The garment quietly supports a life of self-respect.

Mastery of Desire

Most deeply, the Kachhera is understood as a reminder of self-control, especially the discipline of one's desires. Sikh thought identifies certain inner tendencies, including unrestrained lust, as obstacles on the path toward a centered and ethical life. The Kachhera stands for the commitment to live with restraint and faithfulness, channeling one's energies toward honest, constructive ends. In its plain cotton form it brings together practicality, modesty, and moral discipline, three threads woven into the daily life of the committed Sikh.

lessons.title: Kirpan: Justice, Service, and Responsibility
lessons.html:
Kirpan: The Article of Faith Borne for Justice

The fifth article of faith is the Kirpan, a curved blade worn by initiated Sikhs. Of all the Five Ks, the Kirpan is the most often misunderstood, and so it deserves to be explained with particular care and respect.

What the Kirpan Represents

The Kirpan is not carried as a weapon of aggression. It is borne as a solemn reminder of the duty to stand for justice, to protect the vulnerable, and to resist oppression. Its meaning is rooted in compassion: the very name is connected to ideas of mercy and grace. A Sikh wears the Kirpan as a pledge to use strength only in defense of what is right, and never to harm the innocent or to intimidate.

A Pledge of Responsibility

To carry the Kirpan is to accept responsibility for the wellbeing of others. It signals that the wearer is committed to courage in the face of injustice and to the protection of those who cannot protect themselves. In this sense it is closely tied to the saint-soldier ideal: spiritual devotion paired with the readiness to act ethically and bravely in the world.

Respectful Handling

Because the Kirpan is a sacred article of faith rather than an ordinary object, it is treated with reverence. Sikhs handle it respectfully and keep it as a symbol of moral duty, not as a tool of violence. The wearer is expected to embody restraint, dignity, and self-mastery; the Kirpan is meaningful precisely because it is held by someone who is committed to peace and justice. Understood properly, the Kirpan is a reminder of conscience: a call to defend the dignity of all people and to act with both courage and compassion.

lessons.title: Saabat Soorat, the Rehat, and the Amrit Sanchar
lessons.html:
The Wider Framework of Sikh Identity

The Five Ks do not stand alone. They are part of a larger understanding of how a Sikh is meant to live, expressed in three connected ideas: the intact form known as Saabat Soorat, the code of conduct known as the Rehat, and the initiation ceremony known as the Amrit Sanchar.

Saabat Soorat: The Intact Form

Saabat Soorat means the complete or intact form. It describes the practice of keeping the body whole and unaltered, most visibly through unshorn hair, as an expression of acceptance of one's God-given shape. The Five Ks together help maintain this intact form and make it recognizable. The idea behind Saabat Soorat is that the outer person and the inner person should be in harmony, neither hidden nor changed to suit fashion.

The Rehat: A Code of Conduct

The Rehat is the body of guidance describing how a committed Sikh should live. At a high level, it covers daily prayer and remembrance of the Divine, honest work, sharing with others, service to the community, ethical behavior, and the wearing of the articles of faith. The Rehat is best understood not as a list of rules to be feared but as a path that supports a focused, generous, and disciplined life. The Five Ks are among its visible commitments.

The Amrit Sanchar: The Gateway

The Amrit Sanchar is the ceremony of initiation through which a Sikh formally joins the Khalsa. It is a deeply meaningful occasion in which the participant makes a heartfelt commitment to the Sikh way of life, including the keeping of the Five Ks and the living of the Rehat. Through this ceremony the individual takes on the responsibilities of the Khalsa freely and consciously. The Amrit Sanchar is therefore described as the gateway to the committed Sikh identity: it marks the moment when the articles of faith are taken up not as customs but as a lifelong promise.

lessons.title: Identity in Service, Accountability, and the Modern World
lessons.html:
Living the Identity Today

Having studied each of the Five Ks and the framework that surrounds them, we can now consider how a visible Sikh identity functions in daily life, and how it is understood in the modern world.

Identity, Discipline, and Commitment

At its heart, the Sikh identity is about commitment made tangible. The articles of faith are daily reminders that one has promised to live in a particular way: with prayerfulness, honesty, humility, and courage. Because they are worn and maintained every day, they turn abstract ideals into concrete habits. In this sense the identity is a form of spiritual discipline, keeping one's values continually in view.

Service and Accountability

A visible identity also carries social meaning. A person recognizable as a Sikh is, in a sense, publicly accountable. The community and even strangers may look to such a person to act with the integrity the identity represents. This encourages a high standard of conduct and a readiness to help others. The Sikh tradition places great value on selfless service, and the visible identity supports this by reminding the wearer, and signaling to others, that one is committed to the good of the community.

Common Misunderstandings

In the modern world, Sikhs are sometimes misidentified or misunderstood, in part because the articles of faith are unfamiliar to many people. The turban, for example, may be wrongly associated with traditions to which it does not belong, and the Kirpan may be mistaken for a weapon of aggression rather than understood as a sacred reminder of justice and compassion. Clear, respectful education helps correct these misunderstandings.

Continuity and Confidence

Maintaining a distinct identity can require courage in places where it is uncommon, yet many Sikhs find that it brings a deep sense of belonging and purpose. The identity connects the individual to a long heritage and to a worldwide community, while also serving as a personal anchor in a fast-changing world. Understood properly, the Five Ks and the broader articles of faith are not relics of the past but a living expression of devotion, dignity, discipline, and service, carried with quiet confidence into everyday life.

courses.title: Guru Gobind Singh Ji and the Birth of the Khalsa
courses.summary: An exploration of the life of Guru Gobind Singh Ji, the tenth Guru of the Sikhs, set within the turbulent political world of the late seventeenth century. This course traces his early years, the founding of the Khalsa at Anandpur on Vaisakhi 1699, the great sacrifices of his family, the writings associated with him, and the conferral of eternal Guruship upon Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji in 1708. Throughout, documented history is distinguished from cherished tradition, and points of scholarly debate are noted neutrally.

lessons.title: The World Into Which He Was Born
lessons.html:
A Time of Pressure and Hope

Guru Gobind Singh Ji was born in 1666 in Patna, in the eastern region of present-day Bihar, while his father, Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji, the ninth Guru, was traveling through the eastern provinces. The child given the name Gobind Rai entered a world shaped by the expanding power of the Mughal Empire under the emperor Aurangzeb, whose reign brought intensified religious orthodoxy and growing pressure upon communities that did not conform.

By the seventeenth century the Sikh community founded by Guru Nanak Dev Ji had grown into a distinct spiritual movement with its own scripture, institutions, and centers of gathering. Successive Gurus had developed the community's social and devotional life. With this growth came friction with imperial authority, which at times viewed an organized and independent community with suspicion.

The Eastern Years

Tradition holds that the young Gobind Rai spent his earliest years in Patna, where local memory still honors the site, before the family journeyed northward to the Punjab hills. He was raised in an environment of learning, devotion, and martial discipline, and accounts describe his early aptitude for languages, poetry, and the bearing of a leader.

It is worth pausing on a point of method that will recur in this course. Much of what we know about the Gurus comes from a blend of contemporary records, later devotional biographies known as the Janamsakhi and Gurbilas traditions, and oral memory. Where a detail rests primarily on tradition rather than on firmly dated documentation, this course will say so plainly, without diminishing its meaning to the community that preserves it.

lessons.title: The Martyrdom of Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji
lessons.html:
A Sacrifice for the Freedom of Conscience

The single event that most directly shaped the young Guru's path was the martyrdom of his father, Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji, in Delhi in 1675. According to the widely held account preserved within the tradition, a delegation of Kashmiri Pandits sought the ninth Guru's help against forced conversion. Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji took up their cause, standing for the principle that people should be free to follow their own faith.

Summoned to the imperial capital, the Guru refused to abandon his beliefs or to accept conversion under duress. He was executed in Delhi at the site now marked by Gurdwara Sis Ganj Sahib. Sikhs remember him as the protector of the freedom of conscience, one who gave his life not for his own community alone but for the right of others to worship as they chose.

The Weight Passed to a Child

Guru Gobind Singh Ji, then about nine years old, became the tenth Guru upon his father's martyrdom. Tradition recounts that it was the young Guru himself who, when his father weighed how to answer the plea of the Pandits, counseled that no one was more worthy than Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji to make such a stand. Whether read as documented exchange or as treasured memory, the episode frames the inheritance the boy received: a Guruship forged in the conviction that righteousness may demand the highest cost.

This martyrdom forms the essential background to everything that follows. The decisions Guru Gobind Singh Ji would later make, above all the founding of the Khalsa, can be understood as a response to a world in which faith and dignity required defense.

lessons.title: Anandpur and the Years of Preparation
lessons.html:
A Center of Learning and Strength

Guru Gobind Singh Ji established his principal seat at Anandpur Sahib in the foothills of the Punjab. There he gathered scholars, poets, and devotees, and the town became a vibrant center of devotion, learning, and the arts. The Guru himself was a gifted poet and patron of poetry, and many writers worked under his patronage to render heroic and devotional literature into accessible languages.

Alongside this cultural flourishing, the Guru encouraged discipline of body as well as of spirit. The community at Anandpur trained in arms and horsemanship. This was not a turn away from the spiritual teachings of the earlier Gurus but, in the Sikh understanding, an integration of the devotional and the courageous, sometimes expressed in the ideal of the saint-soldier who keeps God in mind while standing ready to defend the weak.

Rising Tensions in the Hills

Anandpur sat among the small kingdoms of the Punjab hill rajas, whose relations with the Guru shifted between alliance and conflict. The growth of a strong, independent community drew the wariness of some neighboring rulers and, in time, of Mughal authority. Several engagements were fought in this period, the most often noted being the battle at Bhangani, near Paonta, early in the Guru's career.

These years of preparation set the stage for the transformative event of 1699. The community was already substantial and disciplined; what the Guru would give it on Vaisakhi was a new form, a new identity, and a new covenant.

lessons.title: Vaisakhi 1699 and the Panj Pyare
lessons.html:
The Gathering at Anandpur

On the festival of Vaisakhi in 1699, Guru Gobind Singh Ji called the community to assemble at Anandpur Sahib. Before the great gathering, in an event remembered with deep reverence, the Guru issued a striking call. Drawn sword in hand, he asked who among them was willing to give his head for his faith.

Tradition recounts that after a tense silence one man rose and offered himself. The Guru led him into a tent, and the assembly heard the sound of a falling blow. He emerged and called for a second head, and a second came forward, and so on until five had offered themselves. The Guru then presented all five alive, in the telling, robed and transformed before the people.

The Five Beloved Ones

These five became known as the Panj Pyare, the Five Beloved Ones. Sikh tradition records their names and the regions and varied social backgrounds from which they came: Bhai Daya Singh, Bhai Dharam Singh, Bhai Himmat Singh, Bhai Mohkam Singh, and Bhai Sahib Singh. That they came from different places and walks of life is itself central to the meaning of the moment, for the new community would dissolve the divisions of caste and origin.

The dramatic narrative of the offered heads is preserved within the tradition and is told in this way across the community. Historians note that the precise inner details rest on the devotional sources; what is firmly established is the public, founding character of the event and the emergence of the Five Beloved Ones as the first members of the new order.

lessons.title: The Amrit Sanchar and the Khalsa
lessons.html:
The Initiation of the Pure

Having called forth the Five Beloved Ones, Guru Gobind Singh Ji initiated them into a new collective body called the Khalsa, a term carrying the sense of being pure and belonging wholly to the Divine. The ceremony of initiation is the Amrit Sanchar. Iron sweets were dissolved in water within an iron vessel and stirred with a double-edged sword, the khanda, while sacred verses were recited. This prepared amrit, the sweetened blessed water, was given to the five.

In a gesture of profound humility honored by Sikhs to this day, the Guru then asked the Five Beloved Ones to initiate him in turn. By receiving amrit from those he had himself initiated, the Guru placed himself within the Khalsa rather than above it, expressing the principle that the Guru and the Khalsa share a single body. From that day the Guru took the name Gobind Singh, and the initiated took the name Singh, meaning lion, while initiated women took the name Kaur.

A Shared Discipline

Membership in the Khalsa came with a way of life. Initiated Sikhs committed themselves to daily prayer, honest living, service to others, and devotion to one God, and they pledged to set aside the divisions and superstitions that the Gurus had long taught against. The Khalsa was conceived as a community of equals, bound by a common faith and a common code rather than by birth.

This lesson describes the ceremony reverently and at a high level. The full and precise observance of Amrit Sanchar is held within the living practice of the community and is approached by Sikhs as a sacred undertaking, not merely as a historical detail.

lessons.title: The Five Ks and the Khalsa Code
lessons.html:
Articles of Faith

Among the most visible features of the Khalsa are the five articles of faith, often called the Five Ks because each begins with the letter K in Punjabi. Together they form an outward expression of the inner commitment made at initiation. Described at a high level, they are: Kesh, uncut hair, kept as it is naturally given and respected as a sign of acceptance of the Divine will; Kangha, a small comb kept in the hair as a reminder of cleanliness and order; Kara, a steel bracelet worn as a sign of restraint and of binding to the truth; Kachhera, a particular undergarment associated with readiness and self-discipline; and Kirpan, a blade carried as a symbol of dignity and the duty to protect the vulnerable.

Each article carries meaning beyond its physical form. The Five Ks are not understood within the tradition as mere costume but as a discipline of identity, marking the wearer as one who has taken vows and who is meant to be recognizable and accountable.

The Code of Conduct

Beyond the Five Ks, the Khalsa observes a broader code of conduct that shapes daily life: keeping the regular cycle of prayer, earning an honest living, sharing with those in need, and abstaining from certain practices that the tradition holds to be harmful. The community has, over time, articulated this discipline in formal statements of conduct.

Because details of observance and the wording of the code have been refined and codified across the centuries since 1699, scholars distinguish between the founding principles set at Anandpur and the later organized formulations. This course presents the Five Ks and the code at the level of their enduring meaning, leaving the fine points of observance to the community's own authoritative guidance.

lessons.title: Sacrifice: The Sahibzade and the Great Battles
lessons.html:
The Siege and Departure from Anandpur

The founding of the Khalsa intensified the pressures on Anandpur. Conflict with surrounding hill rulers and Mughal forces grew, and the town came under prolonged siege. After great hardship the Guru and his followers eventually left Anandpur under assurances of safe passage, assurances that, by the accounts preserved in the tradition, were broken once the community was in the open.

In the crossing of the swollen Sarsa river amid attack, the Guru's family was separated. This separation set in motion the events for which this period is most remembered: the martyrdom of his four sons, known as the Sahibzade, and of his mother.

Chamkaur and the Younger Sahibzade

At Chamkaur, the Guru and a small band of Sikhs, including his two elder sons, made a stand against a vastly larger force from a fortified position. The two elder Sahibzade fell in battle there. The disparity between the few defenders and the surrounding army is central to how Sikhs remember Chamkaur as an emblem of courage against impossible odds.

The two younger Sahibzade, mere children, were captured along with their grandmother. According to the account held within the tradition, they were brought before a local governor and pressed to abandon their faith, and when they refused they were put to death. Their steadfastness at so young an age is honored among the most poignant memories in Sikh history. Mata Gujri Ji, the Guru's mother, also passed away in this period of captivity and grief.

Muktsar and the Forty Liberated Ones

Later, at the battle of Muktsar, a group of Sikhs who had earlier turned away from the Guru returned and fought, and they are remembered as the Chali Mukte, the Forty Liberated Ones, who regained their bond with the Guru through their devotion. Across these battles the historical core, that the Guru faced sustained military conflict and bore the loss of his entire immediate family, is firmly established, while certain narrative details survive chiefly through the devotional sources.

lessons.title: Writings, the Eternal Guruship, and Legacy
lessons.html:
The Guru as Poet and Patron

Guru Gobind Singh Ji was a accomplished poet and a great patron of literature. The body of compositions associated with him and with the poets of his court is gathered in a collection known as the Dasam Granth. This collection contains devotional hymns, narrative and heroic poetry, and other works in several languages.

The Dasam Granth is a subject approached carefully within Sikh scholarship. Certain compositions within it are widely accepted and are recited daily by Sikhs, while questions of authorship and of the inclusion of particular sections have been the subject of respectful study and debate among scholars and within the community. This course notes the collection neutrally, recognizing both its place in Sikh devotional life and the ongoing scholarly discussion surrounding parts of it, without taking a side.

The Eternal Guru

In 1708, at Nanded in present-day Maharashtra, near the end of his life, Guru Gobind Singh Ji made a decision of lasting consequence. He declared that there would be no further human Guru after him. Instead, he conferred the eternal Guruship upon the sacred scripture, Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji, the compilation of the divine word assembled by the earlier Gurus and saints. From that moment the scripture would serve as the living, eternal Guru for all Sikhs, while the collective body of the Khalsa would carry forward the community's life.

Guru Gobind Singh Ji passed away at Nanded in 1708. The site is honored as Hazur Sahib, one of the most revered places in the Sikh world.

A Lasting Inheritance

The legacy of the tenth Guru is woven into the daily identity of Sikhs everywhere. He gave the community the Khalsa, the discipline of the Five Ks, the names Singh and Kaur, and a vision of the saint-soldier devoted to God and to justice. Above all he secured the line of guidance by enthroning the scripture as the eternal Guru. His life, from the martyrdom of his father to the sacrifices of his sons and his own final act of devotion, stands as one of the defining chapters in the history of the Sikh faith.

quiz.q: In what year was Guru Gobind Singh Ji born, and in which city?
options: 1666 in Patna
1675 in Delhi
1699 in Anandpur
1708 in Nanded

quiz.q: Whose martyrdom in 1675 most directly shaped the young Guru's path?
options: Guru Nanak Dev Ji
Guru Arjan Dev Ji
Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji
Guru Amar Das Ji

quiz.q: On which festival, and in what year, was the Khalsa founded at Anandpur?
options: Diwali 1666
Vaisakhi 1699
Vaisakhi 1708
Hola Mohalla 1675

quiz.q: What name is given to the first five Sikhs initiated into the Khalsa?
options: The Chali Mukte
The Sahibzade
The Panj Pyare
The Hill Rajas

quiz.q: What gesture of humility did Guru Gobind Singh Ji make during the first Amrit Sanchar?
options: He refused to take any name
He asked the Five Beloved Ones to initiate him in turn
He gave up his sword
He left Anandpur immediately

quiz.q: Which of the following is one of the Five Ks?
options: Kara, a steel bracelet
A golden crown
A written scroll
A wooden staff

quiz.q: At which battle did the Guru and a small band, including his two elder sons, make a stand against a far larger force?
options: Bhangani
Chamkaur
Muktsar
Nanded

quiz.q: What lasting decision did Guru Gobind Singh Ji make in 1708 at Nanded?
options: He appointed an eleventh human Guru
He disbanded the Khalsa
He conferred eternal Guruship upon Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji
He moved the community back to Patna

courses.title: Guru Nanak Dev Ji: Life and Teachings
courses.summary: An accessible introduction to the life, travels, and core message of Guru Nanak Dev Ji (1469-1539), the founder of the Sikh faith. The course traces his birth at Nankana Sahib, his early life and the revelation at the river, the four Udasis or great journeys, and the foundational teachings of oneness, honest living, sharing, and remembrance of the Divine. It also examines the founding of Kartarpur and the first Sikh community, and the passing of Guruship to Guru Angad. Throughout, the course carefully distinguishes well-documented history from the devotional janamsakhi tradition, presenting both reverently and neutrally.

lessons.title: Birth and Early Life at Nankana Sahib
lessons.html:
A Child of the Punjab

Guru Nanak Dev Ji was born in 1469 in the village of Rai Bhoi di Talwandi, in the Punjab region of what is today Pakistan. The town was later renamed Nankana Sahib in his honor, and it remains one of the most cherished places of pilgrimage for Sikhs around the world. His father, Mehta Kalu, served as an accountant managing the revenue records of the local landlord, and his mother, Mata Tripta, is remembered for her gentleness and devotion.

An Unusual Child

From an early age, the traditional accounts describe Nanak as a contemplative child who asked searching questions about God, ritual, and the meaning of life. His elder sister, Bibi Nanaki, recognized something extraordinary in him and remained one of his earliest and most devoted supporters throughout his life. Many of the well-loved stories from his childhood come to us through the janamsakhis, devotional biographies composed in the generations after his passing. These should be read as treasured tradition rather than as documentary record; historians treat them with care while honoring the spirit they convey.

The Sacred Thread

One frequently retold episode concerns the janeu, the sacred thread that boys of certain communities were expected to wear in a coming-of-age ceremony. According to tradition, the young Nanak declined the ritual thread, asking instead for a thread of compassion, contentment, and truth that would never break or wear out. Whether or not the scene unfolded exactly as recounted, it captures a theme that would define his entire message: the inner reality of a virtuous life matters far more than outward ceremony.

How We Know What We Know

As we begin this study, it is worth naming our sources clearly. The most reliable window into Guru Nanak's mind is his own poetry, preserved in the Guru Granth Sahib. The narrative details of his life come largely from the janamsakhi tradition, which is devotional in purpose. Throughout this course we will note where an account is firmly historical and where it belongs to cherished tradition.

lessons.title: The Revelation at the River
lessons.html:
A Turning Point

As a young man, Guru Nanak married Mata Sulakhni and the couple had two sons, Sri Chand and Lakhmi Das. He took up employment at Sultanpur Lodhi, where his sister Nanaki lived, working in the storehouse of the local governor. He was, by the accounts, honest and diligent in his work even as his heart remained drawn toward the Divine.

The Three Days

The defining event of Guru Nanak's life is traditionally placed at Sultanpur Lodhi. According to the janamsakhi accounts, one morning he entered the waters of the Bein river to bathe and did not emerge for three days. When he returned, he had undergone a profound spiritual experience and spoke words that have echoed through the centuries.

"There Is No Hindu, No Muslim"

His first declaration is traditionally remembered as: na ko hindu na ko musalman - "There is no Hindu, no Muslim." It is important to understand this statement in its proper context. Guru Nanak was not dismissing anyone's faith or community. He was making a deeper point: in the eyes of the One Creator, the labels and divisions that people use to rank and separate one another carry no ultimate weight. What matters is how one lives - whether with truth, humility, and devotion. He spent his life teaching members of every community, and his closest lifelong companion was Bhai Mardana, a Muslim musician.

The Beginning of the Mission

After this experience, Guru Nanak began to share his message in verse and song, often accompanied by Mardana on the rabab, a stringed instrument. This marks the start of his life as a teacher and the genesis of the Sikh tradition, whose very name comes from a word meaning "learner" or "disciple."

lessons.title: The Udasis: Journeys of a Teacher
lessons.html:
Carrying the Message

Rather than settling into a single place, Guru Nanak set out on a series of long journeys known as the Udasis. Tradition counts four major journeys spanning roughly two decades, during which he is said to have traveled astonishing distances on foot to share his message and to engage in dialogue with people of many faiths and walks of life.

The Four Directions

The journeys are traditionally described as reaching out toward the four directions. He is said to have traveled east toward Bengal and Assam, south toward Sri Lanka, north into the Himalayan regions, and west toward the centers of the Islamic world. Along the way he visited temples, mosques, monasteries, and gathering places of holy men, meeting people in their own settings and speaking to their own concerns.

Companionship on the Road

For much of this period, Bhai Mardana accompanied him. Their partnership, with Guru Nanak composing verses and Mardana setting them to music, became a model for how the divine message would be carried through devotional song, a practice that remains central to Sikh worship today.

A Note on the Accounts

The geographic scope of the Udasis as described in tradition is vast, and historians debate the precise routes and the historicity of particular destinations. What is not in doubt is that Guru Nanak was a great traveler who engaged directly with diverse communities. As we look at specific episodes in the next lesson, we will treat them as they have been lovingly recounted, while remembering that many come to us through devotional tradition rather than verified record.

lessons.title: Episodes and Sayings from the Journeys
lessons.html:
Teaching Through Encounter

The janamsakhis preserve many memorable episodes from the Udasis. Each is told to illustrate a teaching, and each rewards reflection regardless of how literally one reads the details. The following are among the most beloved, presented as they are traditionally recounted.

The Direction of Prayer

In one account set near Mecca, Guru Nanak was resting and was rebuked for lying with his feet pointing toward the holy sanctuary. He is said to have gently replied that the questioner should turn his feet in whatever direction God was not. The story is used to teach that the Divine is present everywhere, not confined to a single building or direction.

The True Offering

Another well-known episode involves a wealthy man, Malik Bhago, who hosted a lavish feast, and a poor carpenter named Bhai Lalo, who offered simple food earned through honest labor. According to tradition, Guru Nanak showed that the honest bread of the laborer was sweet while the rich man's food, gained through exploitation, was bitter. The lesson is that the fruit of honest work is purer than wealth gained unjustly.

The Field of Grain

Several stories turn on hospitality, humility, and the dignity of the humble. In each, Guru Nanak consistently elevated sincerity and service over status and show. These narratives, taken together, form a coherent moral vision: live honestly, serve others, and see the Divine in all.

Reading Reverently and Carefully

These episodes are cherished and instructive. At the same time, a careful student notes that they belong to the devotional biography tradition. Their enduring value lies in the teachings they convey, which align closely with the message found in Guru Nanak's own verses.

lessons.title: Core Teachings: Oneness, Naam, and Honest Living
lessons.html:
Ik Onkar: The One

The opening words of the Guru Granth Sahib, composed by Guru Nanak, are Ik Onkar - "One Universal Creator." This phrase is the cornerstone of his entire teaching. There is one Reality underlying all existence, beyond birth and death, beyond fear and enmity, self-existent and known through grace. Recognizing this oneness dissolves the artificial barriers people build between themselves.

Naam: Remembering the Divine

Central to Guru Nanak's path is Naam, the conscious remembrance of the Divine. To dwell on the One with love and attention reorients the whole of life. This practice is sometimes called Naam Japo - lovingly remembering and meditating on the Divine Name. It is not a withdrawal from the world but a way of living in it with awareness and gratitude.

Three Pillars of Daily Life

Guru Nanak distilled spiritual life into three practical principles that a householder could live by every day:

  • Naam Japo - remember the Divine with love and constancy.
  • Kirat Karo - earn your living through honest, dignified work.
  • Vand Chhako - share what you have with others, especially those in need.

A Path for Everyone

Unlike traditions that prized renunciation, Guru Nanak taught that the spiritual life is to be lived in the midst of family and society. One need not abandon the world to find the Divine; rather, one finds the Divine by living truthfully within it. This affirmation of ordinary life as a setting for the sacred is one of his most distinctive contributions.

lessons.title: Equality and the Rejection of Empty Ritual
lessons.html:
One Creator, One Family

If there is one Creator of all, Guru Nanak reasoned, then all people share a common origin and dignity. From this conviction flowed a sustained challenge to the social hierarchies of his time. He rejected the idea that birth into a particular caste makes one person superior or another inferior, and he taught that all stand equal before the Divine.

The Dignity of Women

Guru Nanak spoke directly against the devaluation of women. In a verse from his composition Asa di Var, he asks why one should call inferior the very people who give birth to kings and to all of humanity. This affirmation of the worth of women was striking in his era and became a lasting principle of the tradition he founded.

Beyond Empty Ritual

Guru Nanak consistently distinguished between sincere devotion and mechanical ritual. He questioned practices performed without understanding - bathing at pilgrimage sites to wash away wrongdoing, ritual offerings believed to reach ancestors, fasting and outward austerities pursued for show. His point was not that observance is worthless but that ritual divorced from inner truth and ethical living is hollow.

A Verse Explained

In one well-known image, Guru Nanak observes that if mere washing made one pure, then fish and frogs, ever immersed in water, would be the holiest of all. The verse uses gentle wit to make a serious point: true purity is a matter of the heart and conduct, not of external rites. Across his poetry, the consistent message is that God looks to sincerity, compassion, and honest living rather than to ceremony.

lessons.title: Kartarpur and the First Sikh Community
lessons.html:
Settling at Kartarpur

After his years of travel, Guru Nanak settled in his later life at Kartarpur, a town he established on the banks of the Ravi river. Here he lived as a farmer and householder, working the land while teaching. Kartarpur became the first organized Sikh community, a living demonstration of the principles he had preached on his journeys.

Sangat: The Gathered Community

At Kartarpur, followers gathered to sing the divine praises and to learn. This Sangat, or holy congregation, became a defining institution of Sikh life. Worship centered on devotional singing of the verses Guru Nanak composed, and the community provided a setting in which the teachings could be lived out together rather than pursued in isolation.

Pangat and Langar: Eating as Equals

One of the most enduring institutions to take shape was the practice of Pangat and Langar. Langar is the community kitchen that prepares and serves free food to all; Pangat refers to everyone sitting together in a row to eat, regardless of caste, status, wealth, or background. In a society sharply divided by such distinctions, sitting and eating as equals was a powerful enactment of the teaching of oneness. Langar continues today in every Sikh gurdwara around the world.

A Model in Action

Kartarpur was, in effect, Guru Nanak's teachings made visible. Honest work in the fields embodied Kirat Karo; the free kitchen embodied Vand Chhako; the gathered congregation embodied Naam Japo. The community he built there set the pattern that later Gurus would develop and expand.

lessons.title: The Passing of Guruship to Guru Angad
lessons.html:
Choosing a Successor

As Guru Nanak's life drew toward its close, the question of continuity arose. Rather than passing leadership to either of his own sons, he chose a devoted follower named Bhai Lehna on the basis of his humility, service, and understanding of the teachings. This decision established a foundational principle: spiritual succession would rest on merit and devotion, not on family inheritance.

Becoming Angad

Guru Nanak renamed Bhai Lehna as Angad, a name traditionally understood to mean "a part of my own body," signifying the unity of the Guruship across the change in person. Tradition holds that the same divine light was passed from one to the next, so that the message would continue undimmed. Guru Angad would go on to develop the Gurmukhi script in which the Sikh scriptures are written and to strengthen the institutions Guru Nanak had begun.

The Passing in 1539

Guru Nanak Dev Ji passed away at Kartarpur in 1539. A cherished tradition recounts that his Hindu and Muslim followers each wished to perform the last rites according to their own customs, and that the dispute itself testified to how deeply he had been loved across communities. The story is told to underscore the unifying spirit of his life.

An Enduring Legacy

Guru Nanak founded a tradition rooted in the oneness of the Divine, the equality of all people, honest living, sharing, and loving remembrance. The community at Kartarpur, the institutions of Sangat and Langar, the body of sacred verse, and a line of succession built on devotion together formed a foundation that nine Gurus after him would build upon. His message endures wherever Sikhs gather to remember the One and to serve all.

quiz.q: In what year and place was Guru Nanak Dev Ji born?
options: 1469 at Rai Bhoi di Talwandi, later renamed Nankana Sahib
1539 at Kartarpur
1499 at Sultanpur Lodhi
1430 at Amritsar

quiz.q: What is the best understanding of Guru Nanak's saying traditionally rendered "There is no Hindu, no Muslim"?
options: He was founding a new political movement against both groups
Before the One Creator, the divisive labels people use to rank each other carry no ultimate weight
He believed both religions should be abolished
He was rejecting all forms of worship

quiz.q: What name is given to Guru Nanak's series of great journeys?
options: The Langars
The Sangats
The Udasis
The Pangats

quiz.q: Who was Guru Nanak's lifelong musical companion on his travels?
options: Bhai Lehna
Sri Chand
Malik Bhago
Bhai Mardana, a Muslim musician

quiz.q: Which three principles summarize Guru Nanak's guidance for daily life?
options: Naam Japo, Kirat Karo, Vand Chhako
Fasting, pilgrimage, renunciation
Wealth, status, ritual
Silence, solitude, self-denial

quiz.q: What does the practice of Pangat and Langar embody in the Sikh community?
options: A strict caste seating order during meals
All people sitting together as equals to share free food, regardless of status
A private meal reserved for religious leaders
An annual festival of fasting

quiz.q: How should the janamsakhi accounts of Guru Nanak's life be understood?
options: As verified legal documents from his lifetime
As writings by Guru Nanak himself
As devotional biographies treasured for their teachings, distinct from documented history
As accounts that historians fully reject as worthless

quiz.q: To whom did Guru Nanak pass the Guruship, and on what basis?
options: To his eldest son, by family inheritance
To the wealthiest follower, to fund the community
To a local ruler, for political protection
To Bhai Lehna, renamed Guru Angad, chosen for his humility, service, and devotion

courses.title: Introduction to Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji
courses.summary: A college-level introduction to Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji, the eternal living Guru of the Sikhs. The course examines how the scripture was compiled by Guru Arjan Dev Ji and completed under Guru Gobind Singh Ji, the community of voices it gathers (Gurus, Bhagats, and Bhatts), its arrangement by Raag, its script and language, and the doctrine of Shabad Guru. Drawing on scholarship by Sahib Singh, Pashaura Singh, and J. S. Grewal, students learn to approach the Granth with both academic care and reverence, and to begin their own study with informed guidance.
outcomes: Explain why Sikhs regard Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji as the eternal living Guru rather than only a sacred book.
Describe the compilation of the Adi Granth by Guru Arjan Dev Ji in 1604 and its completion under Guru Gobind Singh Ji.
Identify the principal communities of contributors, Gurus, Bhagats, and Bhatts, and explain the message of equality their inclusion conveys.
Analyze how arrangement by Raag shapes the reading and singing of Gurbani.
Discuss the script and the multilingual character of the text and what they reveal about the scripture's inclusiveness.
Articulate the doctrine of Shabad Guru and its bearing on how Sikhs revere and read the Granth.

terms.m: the sacred utterance of the Guru recorded in scripture
the Adi Granth, the first compiled volume of Sikh scripture
the divine Word, the spiritual teaching that liberates
the melodic framework by which most of the scripture is arranged
a devotee saint whose verses are included in the scripture
a bardic poet whose praises of the Gurus appear in the scripture
the script in which the scripture is written
an unbroken continuous reading of the entire scripture

references: Sahib Singh. Sri Guru Granth Sahib Darpan. Jalandhar: Raj Publishers.
Singh, Pashaura. The Guru Granth Sahib: Canon, Meaning and Authority. New Delhi: Oxford University Press, 2000.
Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1998.
Singh, Pashaura, and Louis E. Fenech, eds. The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2014.

lessons.title: The Eternal Living Guru
lessons.summary: Why Sikhs regard the scripture as a living Guru, and how that belief shapes its treatment.
lessons.html:
Full course contents
  1. The Eternal Living Guru
  2. Compilation: The Adi Granth of 1604
  3. The Final Form and Bhagat Bani
  4. The Community of Voices: Gurus, Bhagats, and Bhatts
  5. Organization by Raag
  6. Script, Language, and the Doctrine of Shabad Guru

The Eternal Living Guru

For Sikhs, Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji is not merely a holy book or a historical record. It is regarded as the living, eternal Guru, the embodied spiritual presence that guides the community. This understanding shapes everything about how the scripture is kept, carried, and consulted (Singh 2000).

The line of human Gurus began with Guru Nanak Dev Ji and continued through nine successors. In 1708, Guru Gobind Singh Ji, the tenth Guru, conferred eternal Guruship upon the scripture, so that thereafter Sikhs would turn to the ਗੁਰਬਾਣੀ (the Guru's sacred word) as their teacher and source of spiritual authority (Grewal 1998).

Reverence in practice

Because the Granth is treated as a living Guru, it is shown the respect one would offer a revered teacher. It is placed upon a raised platform, covered with fine cloths, and given a place of honor. People remove their shoes and cover their heads in its presence, and it is never set on the bare ground.

  • The honor is directed to the teaching, not to paper or ink.
  • The customs express a student's love for a cherished guide.
  • The volume is cherished because it houses the ਸ਼ਬਦ (divine Word) that leads a seeker toward the Divine.
Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge, 1998. Singh, Pashaura. The Guru Granth Sahib: Canon, Meaning and Authority. Oxford, 2000.

lessons.title: Compilation: The Adi Granth of 1604
lessons.summary: How Guru Arjan Dev Ji gathered and ordered the scripture at Amritsar in 1604.
lessons.html:
Compilation: The Adi Granth of 1604

The first authoritative compilation of Sikh scripture was the ਆਦਿ ਗ੍ਰੰਥ (the Adi Granth, the first volume), prepared by Guru Arjan Dev Ji, the fifth Guru. He gathered the compositions of the earlier Gurus, together with the writings of devotees from diverse backgrounds, and arranged them into a single, carefully ordered volume (Singh 2000).

This work was completed in 1604 at Amritsar. The original handwritten volume is traditionally associated with the scribe Bhai Gurdas Ji, who recorded the verses under Guru Arjan Dev Ji's guidance. Once finished, the Adi Granth was installed in the Harmandir Sahib, marking the scripture's place at the heart of community life (Grewal 1998).

Why a fixed canon mattered

In an age before printing, sacred verses could be altered or imitated as they passed from hand to hand. By creating a verified collection, the Guru protected the integrity of the ਗੁਰਬਾਣੀ (the Guru's word) for future generations (Singh 2000). The Adi Granth thus stands as a milestone: a unified scripture, organized with great care, that brought generations of spiritual insight into one accessible body of teaching.

Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge, 1998. Singh, Pashaura. The Guru Granth Sahib: Canon, Meaning and Authority. Oxford, 2000.

lessons.title: The Final Form and Bhagat Bani
lessons.summary: The completion of the scripture under Guru Gobind Singh Ji and the meaning of including saints' verses.
lessons.html:
The Final Form and Bhagat Bani

The scripture reached its final form in the early eighteenth century. Guru Gobind Singh Ji added the compositions of Guru Tegh Bahadur Ji, the ninth Guru, who had not been included in the earlier Adi Granth. With these additions, the volume became complete (Singh and Fenech 2014).

Having completed the scripture, Guru Gobind Singh Ji in 1708 conferred upon it the status of eternal Guru, ending the succession of human Gurus and giving the Granth its enduring role as the perpetual spiritual guide of the Sikhs (Grewal 1998).

The inclusion of Bhagat Bani

A remarkable feature of the scripture is the inclusion of ਭਗਤ (devotee saint) compositions, the writings of saints who were not Sikh Gurus. Among them are Kabir, Namdev, Ravidas, and Sheikh Farid, figures who came from varied regions, faiths, and social stations. Their verses sit alongside those of the Gurus, woven into the same musical order (Singh 2000).

This inclusion carries deep meaning. By honoring the words of saints from different communities, the scripture affirms that spiritual truth is not the property of any single tradition or social class. It expresses the teaching that the Divine speaks through sincere seekers everywhere, and that all people share equal dignity before the One (Singh and Fenech 2014).

Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge, 1998. Singh, Pashaura, and Louis E. Fenech, eds. The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies. Oxford, 2014.

lessons.title: The Community of Voices: Gurus, Bhagats, and Bhatts
lessons.summary: The several communities of contributors and the message of equality their shared pages convey.
lessons.html:
The Community of Voices: Gurus, Bhagats, and Bhatts

The voices gathered in Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji come from several sources. First are the Sikh Gurus themselves, whose compositions form the core of the scripture and share a single spiritual vision even as they were written across more than two centuries (Singh 2000).

Second are the ਭਗਤ (devotee saints) introduced earlier. Third are the ਭੱਟ (bardic poets), whose short compositions praise the Gurus and affirm the truth of their teaching, adding another layer of voices to the chorus of the scripture (Singh and Fenech 2014).

Selected contributors

ContributorBackgroundEmphasis of the verses
KabirWeaver by tradeInner devotion over empty ceremony
RavidasFrom a community treated as low in the social orderA society without hierarchy
Sheikh FaridRevered Muslim SufiHumility and longing for the Divine
NamdevDevotee saintLoving devotion in heartfelt language

That such varied contributors share the same pages is itself a teaching. Differences of caste, creed, region, and profession dissolve within the Granth, demonstrating through its very composition the equality at the heart of the Sikh path (Grewal 1998).

Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge, 1998. Singh, Pashaura. The Guru Granth Sahib: Canon, Meaning and Authority. Oxford, 2000.

lessons.title: Organization by Raag
lessons.summary: How arrangement by musical measure shapes the reading and singing of Gurbani.
lessons.html:
Organization by Raag

Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji is organized in a distinctive way: most of its contents are arranged according to ਰਾਗ (musical measure). A Raag is a melodic framework, each associated with a particular mood, time, or feeling (Singh 2000).

Within the scripture, the compositions are grouped under their respective Raags rather than by author or chronology. Under each Raag, the verses of the Gurus generally appear first, followed by those of the Bhagats. This ordering reflects the conviction that the ਗੁਰਬਾਣੀ (the Guru's word) is meant to be sung; the practice of singing the verses, Kirtan, is a central form of Sikh worship (Singh and Fenech 2014).

Framing compositions

  • The scripture opens with the Japji Sahib and certain daily prayers.
  • It closes with concluding compositions, including a section of couplets.
  • These framing portions hold a special place in daily devotional life.

The choice to organize by Raag underscores an important idea: the teaching is not only read with the mind but felt through music that touches the heart. Melody and meaning work together to draw the listener toward contemplation of the Divine (Singh 2000).

Singh, Pashaura. The Guru Granth Sahib: Canon, Meaning and Authority. Oxford, 2000. Singh, Pashaura, and Louis E. Fenech, eds. The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies. Oxford, 2014.

lessons.title: Script, Language, and the Doctrine of Shabad Guru
lessons.summary: The script, the multilingual character of the text, and the central doctrine that the Word itself is Guru.
lessons.html:
Script, Language, and the Doctrine of Shabad Guru

Sri Guru Granth Sahib Ji is written in the ਗੁਰਮੁਖੀ (Gurmukhi script). Its name suggests speech from the mouth of the Guru, and it is closely associated with the Punjabi language (Grewal 1998).

While the script is uniform, the language is rich and varied. It draws on Punjabi together with several related tongues and dialects of the wider region, reflecting the diverse contributors. This linguistic variety is not an obstacle but a reflection of the scripture's inclusiveness: just as it gathers voices from different communities, it embraces words from different speech traditions, all rendered in a single accessible script (Singh 2000).

The doctrine of Shabad Guru

At the center of Sikh understanding lies the idea of the Shabad Guru. The ਸ਼ਬਦ (divine Word) is the sacred utterance that conveys divine wisdom. To say that the Guru is the Shabad is to say that the true teacher is the spiritual Word itself, not a physical form (Singh and Fenech 2014). This idea reaches back to Guru Nanak Dev Ji and was affirmed when Guru Gobind Singh Ji vested the Guruship in the scripture.

Understanding the Shabad Guru clarifies why Sikhs revere the Granth so deeply yet do not worship its material substance. The honor belongs to the Word, the wisdom that liberates. The Shabad is also meant to be lived: reading and singing the Bani is intended to cultivate humility, remembrance of the Divine, and ethical conduct (Singh 2000).

Reverence and beginning one's study

In the Gurdwara the volume rests on a raised throne beneath a canopy, attended by a Granthi who opens, reads, and respectfully closes it according to established custom. One cherished practice is the ਅਖੰਡ ਪਾਠ (unbroken continuous reading) of the entire scripture. For a beginner, approach with covered head, clean hands, and a settled mind; begin with shorter, well-known compositions; and rely on trustworthy translations, reading a little regularly for reflection rather than rushing (Singh and Fenech 2014).

Grewal, J. S. The Sikhs of the Punjab. Cambridge, 1998. Singh, Pashaura, and Louis E. Fenech, eds. The Oxford Handbook of Sikh Studies. Oxford, 2014.
